name: Panama Canal
summary: The Panama Canal is one of the world's biggest engineering wonders — a 51-mile shortcut that lets your ship sail from the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean in about 10 hours. Giant locks lift the whole ship 26 metres up into a jungle lake, then lower it down the other side. Watch carefully: the trees on the banks hide howler monkeys, sloths, and even crocodiles!
funFacts: Without the canal, ships have to sail all the way around South America's Cape Horn — that's about 13,000 km extra, with stormy seas and icebergs. The canal is a massive shortcut.
The canal opened in 1914 after more than 30 years of digging, sweating and setbacks. About 56,000 people worked on the American build — it was one of the hardest jobs ever attempted.
Your ship is lifted 26 metres up (as high as an 8-storey building) using only gravity and water — no pumps! Each lock fills with millions of litres flowing down from Gatun Lake.
Gatun Lake, the giant lake your ship sails across, is artificial — humans made it in 1913 by damming a river. When it was finished it was the biggest human-made lake on Earth.
In 2016 huge new locks opened — Agua Clara and Cocoli — built for enormous 'Neopanamax' ships. Their gates slide sideways like sliding doors instead of swinging open.
thingsToDo: Get up early (around 6am) to watch your ship enter the Agua Clara Locks on the Atlantic side — see the sliding gates close and millions of litres of water lift you up.
Sail across Gatun Lake and grab binoculars: scan the jungle islands like Barro Colorado for howler monkeys, sloths, toucans, iguanas and even crocodiles in the water.
Squeeze through the Culebra Cut — the canal's narrowest section, carved straight through a mountain — and pass under the tall, white Centennial Bridge.
Watch your ship step down through the Pedro Miguel and Cocoli Locks on the Pacific side, dropping back to ocean level one giant water-elevator at a time.
Listen for ship horns and tugboats: little tugs guide your big ship through the new locks. Wave at the workers — they often wave back!
After sunset, look out for the lit-up Bridge of the Americas and the Panama City skyline glittering on the Pacific side as you finish the transit around 6pm.

localWord.meaning: Panamanian slang for 'What's up?' — a friendly hello between kids and friends

images.alt: A huge container ship squeezes through the narrow Miraflores Locks on the Panama Canal.
A giant NeoPanamax ship rises inside the brand-new Agua Clara Locks built in 2016.
The tall white Centennial Bridge soars over the canal beside jungle-covered Gold Hill.
Little train engines called mules pull a big ship through the Gatun Locks.
The canal winds through steep jungle hills at the famous Culebra Cut near the Chagres River.
